Phenomenology & the Brentano School

G. F. Stout

1860 – 1944

A British Brentanian: presentation, apprehension, and the structure of experience.

G. F. Stout’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as a layered system. At the psychological level, it is an empirical, analytic, developmental account of psychical process, organized around attention, conation, belief, affect, memory, and object-reference. At the epistemological level, it is a realism about the objectivity of thought: what we think about is not reducible to a private mental content. At the metaphysical level, especially in Mind and Matter and God and Nature, it becomes an anti-materialist, anti-Cartesian philosophy of the embodied self, culminating in the claim that mind is fundamental in the universe and that Nature requires grounding in a universal Mind. The result is not Cartesian dualism, not materialism, not behaviorism, and not a simple British-idealist dissolution of the physical world into experience. It is a distinctive philosophy of embodied, intentional, conative mind.

1. Historical and intellectual location

George Frederick Stout was both a philosopher and a psychologist. He became editor of Mind in 1891, held Cambridge posts in moral sciences, became Wilde Reader in Mental Philosophy at Oxford, and then Professor of Logic and Metaphysics at St Andrews from 1903 to 1936. His major psychological works include Analytic Psychology and Manual of Psychology; his later Gifford-based metaphysical works are Mind and Matter and God and Nature. The Gifford Lectures site describes the first series as treating the metaphysical difficulties surrounding mind and the material world, and the second as addressing the ultimate nature of the all-inclusive universe and the status and destiny of human beings within it.

Stout belongs to a crucial transitional generation. He stands between British empiricist associationism, British idealism, the emerging scientific psychology of the late nineteenth century, Brentano’s descriptive psychology, and early analytic philosophy. Modern historical scholarship has increasingly treated him as one of the psychological sources of early analytic philosophy: he taught Moore and Russell, and his distinction between act, content, and object helped shape the background against which Moore and Russell moved from idealism toward analytic realism.

The importance of Stout’s role is not merely biographical. Brentano’s intentionality thesis helped reconfigure the study of mind around the directedness of mental acts, and Stout was one of the main English mediators of this Brentanian line. Elizabeth Valentine describes Stout as a key figure in transmitting Brentano’s influence into British philosophy, especially around analysis, reference, intentionality, meaning, and the shift from idealism to realism. Stout’s version of this inheritance was not a simple repetition of Brentano. He distinguished Brentano’s use of “object” as mental content from the Kantian use of “object” as that to which the content refers, and this distinction remained crucial for his whole philosophy of mind.

That historical fact matters because it helps explain Stout’s philosophical originality. He is not merely an introspective psychologist whose work became obsolete once experimental psychology matured. He is one of the thinkers through whom nineteenth-century philosophical psychology became a source for twentieth-century debates about intentionality, judgment, propositions, realism, psychologism, and mind-body relations.

2. The method of psychology: analytic, empirical, developmental

Stout’s psychology begins from ordinary experience, but it is not casual introspection. In the preface to Analytic Psychology, he says that psychology is already becoming pluralized into distinct approaches: physiology, mental pathology, ethnology, psychophysical experiment, and the older English method associated with Hobbes, Locke, Berkeley, Hume, Bain, and others. He does not reject the newer methods; he insists that the traditional analytic method has its own evidential basis and can be checked against the results of other methods.

The task of analytic psychology is to impose systematic order on the facts of mental life disclosed in ordinary experience. Stout says that this branch of psychology “describe[s], analyse[s], and arrange[s]” the processes of developed consciousness; genetic or synthetic psychology, by contrast, traces the evolution of mind from lower to higher forms. His own order of inquiry is methodological: before one can reconstruct the genesis of mind, one must analyze the mature forms of mental process that provide the clues for genetic treatment.

This gives Stout a two-level psychology. First, there is analytic or descriptive psychology: the taxonomy and internal articulation of consciousness as we encounter it. Second, there is genetic or developmental psychology: the account of how those forms arise. In the introduction to Analytic Psychology, he defines psychology as the science of the development of mind and emphasizes that development involves accumulated modification: the products of past process persist and become the basis of later change.

In The Groundwork of Psychology, Stout gives a compact formulation of the same view. Psychology is to give a systematic and coherent account of the flow of psychical process in its forms, phases, stages, and conditions. This requires description, generalization, and explanation; description itself includes analysis, because complex processes must be decomposed into distinguishable constituents and forms of combination.

The important point is that Stout’s psychology is neither reductionist physiology nor merely private introspection. It is empirical, but the empirical facts include the experienced organization of consciousness, the persistence of dispositions, the development of habits, the operation of attention, the directedness of thought, and the objective reference of cognition. It is a philosophical psychology in the strong sense: psychology is not yet separated from questions about knowledge, agency, selfhood, and the metaphysics of mind.

3. Psychical process and psychical dispositions

Stout does not identify mind with a succession of isolated conscious states. He treats mental life as a flow of psychical process supported by dispositions. This is one of the central doctrines needed for a comprehensive reconstruction of his philosophy of mind.

In Analytic Psychology, Stout argues that past experience leaves persistent after-effects, even when the corresponding conscious episodes have ceased. These after-effects are needed to explain retentiveness, memory, habit, acquired tendencies, and the re-emergence of earlier modes of consciousness. He calls them psychical dispositions when viewed from the psychological side, and physiological dispositions when viewed from the side of nervous organization.

This dispositional view is decisive. Mind is not exhausted by what is occurrently conscious. The conscious field at a given moment is only a selection from a larger structured system of retained capacities, tendencies, and possibilities of revival. Stout therefore has a non-occurrent conception of the mental: dispositions are not experiences presently in consciousness, but they are indispensable to explaining the actual course of consciousness.

In The Groundwork of Psychology, Stout sharpens the point. Dispositions are first known by how they operate in determining psychical process. Considered psychologically, they are “permanent possibilities” of psychical process; but a mere possibility must be founded in actual existence. One possible metaphysical account is to call such dispositions unconscious modifications of a subject or soul, and Stout says he has nothing to object to in that as a possible ultimate truth. But for psychology, the soul is not an independently useful explanatory posit; for the psychologist, “soul” is virtually another name for the total system of psychical processes and dispositions. The more useful clue is that psychical dispositions and processes have physiological correlates in nervous tissue. Stout explicitly denies simple identity, but says that, within limits, psychology may treat the psychical and physiological dispositions as corresponding closely enough for explanatory purposes.

This is a subtle position. Stout is not a materialist, because he does not reduce the psychical disposition to the brain state. He is not a pure spiritualist in psychology, because he refuses to make the soul an explanatory object for empirical psychology. He is not a Cartesian dualist, because he does not begin with a self-subsisting immaterial substance facing an external body. His working psychological ontology is process-plus-disposition: the mind is an organized, developing, dispositional flow of psychical life.

4. Intentionality and objective reference

Stout’s account of mind is deeply intentionalist. Mental life is not merely a stream of sensations, feelings, or images; it is structured by reference to objects. His engagement with Brentano is central here.

Stout accepts Brentano’s methodological insight that the ultimate divisions of mental function should be grounded in differences in the way consciousness refers to objects. Brentano’s triad is presentation, judgment or belief, and interest or liking. Stout presents this as a major advance over merely enumerating faculties such as seeing, hearing, feeling, desiring, and so forth.

But Stout also corrects Brentano. He distinguishes content from object. A content or presentation is not the same as the object to which thought refers. This is the point that makes Stout historically important for analytic realism. He argues that the object of thought is not a content of finite consciousness. If the object exists in the sense meant or intended by the thinker, it exists independently of the thinker’s consciousness; and the same applies, mutatis mutandis, to its non-existence. This allows him to explain how we can think of absent, possible, non-existent, and even absurd objects without making them private mental episodes.

The upshot is anti-psychologistic. Meaning, judgment, and objectivity cannot be reduced to subjective mental contents. Thought is psychological as an act, but what thought is about is not thereby made psychological. This is why Stout matters for Moore and Russell. The Leiden summary of Maria van der Schaar’s work states that Moore and Russell’s theory of judgment and proposition must be understood against the background of Stout’s psychological account of judgment and his act/content/object distinction. Valentine similarly notes that for Moore, Russell, and Stout, the objects of thought and judgment, not psychological contents, form the meanings of terms.

Stout’s intentionalism is therefore not merely a thesis about “aboutness.” It is a structural account of the mind: mental acts are directed, but their objects are not swallowed into the act. This makes his psychology compatible with realism about knowledge, truth, and reference.

5. Noetic synthesis: mental unity is not mere association

Stout’s philosophy of mind also contains a powerful critique of associationist atomism. Against the idea that mental order is merely the linking of ideas by contiguity, resemblance, or habit, he introduces the notion of noetic synthesis.

By noetic synthesis, Stout means the union of presentational elements through their reference to a single object. Percepts, ideas, and concepts become psychical units because they are directed toward something perceived, conceived, or thought of. Their unity is not just a mechanical association of ingredients; it is constituted by objective reference.

This account scales upward. In developed consciousness, the mind is organized into systems of noetic units: partial apprehensions are subordinated to more comprehensive apprehensions, and these to still more comprehensive wholes. A percept of a spatial thing includes apprehensions of parts and sensible qualities; an idea in a train of thought includes subordinate ideas and relations that give it significance within the larger topic. Stout compares the organization to an army: the unity of a group depends on a higher-order organizing function, not merely the physical juxtaposition of soldiers.

This leads to a proto-holistic account of thought. Stout distinguishes association from noetic synthesis by showing how a train of thought is partly controlled by the central topic of the whole series. Association operates when the next idea is determined by the last special idea that emerged; noetic synthesis operates when the development is governed by the central idea of the whole topic.

The philosophical significance is substantial. Stout rejects the view that mental organization is bottom-up concatenation alone. Whole-apprehension can guide the emergence of parts. The structure of thought is teleological, object-directed, and topic-governed. This anticipates later themes in Gestalt psychology, phenomenology, and cognitive theories of top-down organization, although Stout’s terminology and metaphysical setting are his own.

6. Attention as object-reference and mental activity

Attention is one of the hinge notions in Stout’s system because it connects intentionality, activity, and mental organization. Attention is not merely the passive presence of a vivid idea. It is a directed mental activity.

Stout says that attention is a mode in which consciousness refers to an object. Thought, considered abstractly, is reference to an object; attention is the direction of thought to a special object in preference to others. But attention is also a causal mental activity: it maintains and develops the apprehension of an object while relatively excluding others.

This makes attention central to his philosophy of mind. Attention is not just one mental episode among others; it is the active principle by which mental life is organized around objects, topics, and purposes. It is also the bridge between cognition and conation. We attend because we are interested, trying, seeking, examining, remembering, comparing, or acting. Attention is therefore both cognitive and practical.

The point also reinforces Stout’s anti-associationism. If attention can maintain and develop an apprehension, then the course of consciousness is not wholly explained by passive associative sequences. Mental activity has organizing power.

7. Conation, will, and active tendency

Stout’s philosophy of mind is conative at its core. “Conation” means striving, appetency, tendency toward an end, or directed mental activity. He treats desire, volition, attention, practical interest, and much of cognition as bound up with conative structure.

In Analytic Psychology, Stout argues that cognitive combinations are not external machinery inserted between felt need and gratification. Rather, the grouping of cognitions is the way conation grows and develops. A vague craving becomes a specific desire; means become objects of desire; partial tendencies may detach from an original end and acquire relative independence.

This is a deep psychological thesis. Desire is not a bare impulse externally guided by cognition. Cognition differentiates conation from within. To want an end is, in many cases, to want the means; to pursue a means is to develop a more articulated conative system. Human practical life is built out of acquired systems of tendencies that began in more primitive needs but become transformed into complex interests, habits, projects, and forms of conduct.

In Mind and Matter, active tendency becomes central to Stout’s account of physical existence and embodied agency. He says that what gives motor process the character of action is the presence of will, taking “will” broadly as conation or appetency. Voluntary action is not merely a sequence of sensations accompanying bodily movement; it is experienced as initiated striving.

This also explains why Stout cannot accept epiphenomenalism. Mental life, especially conation, is not an inert accompaniment of neural process. It is experienced as active, purposive, and organizing. His critique of materialism repeatedly returns to the common-sense fact of mental agency: volition is not plausibly treated as a mere by-product that does no work.

8. Belief, imagination, and practical orientation

Stout’s treatment of belief also links mind to activity. He discusses the Bainian thesis that belief is a condition of activity: what we believe, we act upon. But he does not simply reduce belief to action. Instead, he argues that a full account must combine belief as a condition of activity with the way activity is limited or restricted by belief and doubt. Certainty, uncertainty, hesitation, and practical confidence all have conative significance.

The psychological importance is clear: belief is not merely a static representation. It structures conduct. Doubt obstructs action; assurance facilitates action; belief connects means and ends. Stout’s analysis thereby anticipates later pragmatist and action-oriented accounts of belief, while remaining more realist than many pragmatist accounts because the object believed is not reduced to its practical role.

Imagination similarly belongs to the economy of action. In the later account of the embodied self, Stout treats the “imagining and desiring self” as still essentially embodied, even when dealing with possible or future situations rather than the immediately present environment. Imagination is therefore not a ghostly inner theatre detached from action; it is a mode of embodied practical orientation toward absent, possible, or future objects.

9. Pleasure, pain, affect, and value

Stout’s account of affect is also conative. Pleasure and pain are not mere sensory atoms added to cognition. In Analytic Psychology, he argues that the contrast between pleasure and pain coincides with the contrast between free and impeded progress toward an end. Unimpeded progress is pleasant; activity that is thwarted, obstructed, or retarded is painful in proportion to the intensity, complexity, and degree of hindrance involved.

This doctrine reappears in Mind and Matter. There Stout says that pleasure and pain are ways in which we immediately experience active tendency as successful or unsuccessful, furthered or thwarted. He acknowledges that some psychologists deny this for sensuous pleasures and pains, such as toothache or sweetness, but he still maintains that pleasure-pain requires no distinct physiological correlate over and above the relevant sensation and active tendency.

Affect is therefore bound to agency and value. Pleasure and pain make possible appreciation of good and evil because they disclose the success or failure of active tendencies. In the final metaphysical movement of Mind and Matter, Stout explicitly says that the individual self includes feelings of pleasure and pain that make possible appreciation of values and the distinction between good and evil.

This gives Stout a unified picture: cognition presents and organizes objects; conation strives toward ends; affect registers the furthering or thwarting of active tendency; value arises within the affective-conative life of embodied selves.

10. Language, thought, and propositions

Stout’s psychology of language is not merely linguistic behaviorism. Language is a tool for controlling thought. In Analytic Psychology, he argues that spoken words have a high degree of reproductive efficacy: they can call up associated ideas more vividly and reliably than images alone. Because the production of a word-percept lies within the subject’s own power, language gives the subject command over associated imagery; internal speech can perform a similar function.

This is a psychologically rich account of language as cognitive instrumentation. Words stabilize, revive, organize, and direct thought. They are not merely external signs; they become tools of attention, memory, imagination, and conceptual control.

Stout’s later account of propositions deepens the point. In “The Nature of Universals and Propositions,” he says that a proposition is something set before the mind as the object of subjective processes such as questioning, doubting, asserting, supposing, practical deliberation, and decision. He also emphasizes that there is knowledge without explicit judgment: much is simply taken for granted in the background from which propositions emerge. A proposition becomes explicit when something is apprehended as a possible alternative.

This account is significant for philosophy of mind because it refuses to identify all cognition with explicit judgment. The mind operates with a vast background of taken-for-granted practical and cognitive orientation. Propositions emerge from that background when alternatives become salient, especially under questioning, doubt, supposition, deliberation, or conflict. This is a sophisticated theory of the relation between tacit cognition, explicit judgment, and practical possibility.

Stout’s proposition theory also connects with his realism. Possibilities are not mere inventions of the understanding; they belong to the objective situation as alternatives confronting a knowing and willing mind. A proposition is true when it corresponds to the realized alternative, false when it corresponds to an unrealized alternative.

11. Perception and knowledge of the physical world

Stout’s account of perception is neither sense-datum phenomenalism nor naïve materialism. He thinks physical existence is known through the inseparable cooperation of sense and activity.

In Mind and Matter, he argues that immediate experience is a “thin slice” of the world process and cannot be conceived in isolation. It is incomplete in such a way that knowing it involves knowing it as continued into a wider world process. This incompleteness is unintelligible unless activity is included.

Physical existence, as primarily known, has two inseparable aspects. On the sensory side, we know bodies through material characters derived from sense-experience. On the dynamical side, we apprehend our bodies and external objects as active and passive in relation to one another. Effort against resistance discloses bodily action and external resistance in a way that cannot be reduced to sensation alone.

This is an embodied realism. The physical world is not inferred from private ideas as a hidden cause. Nor is it reducible to the immediate content of sensation. Physical existence is apprehended through sense, active tendency, resistance, bodily agency, and the continuity of immediate experience with a wider world process.

That view also explains why Stout’s philosophy of mind cannot be detached from his philosophy of matter. Mind is embodied from the start; body is not a later inference from private consciousness; the external world is disclosed in the same field in which the self is aware of itself as bodily active and passive.

12. The embodied self

The doctrine of the embodied self is the centerpiece of Stout’s mature philosophy of mind. It is his answer to Cartesian dualism, reductive materialism, pure spiritualism, and much of the traditional mind-body problem.

Stout’s diagnosis is that modern philosophy has often allowed physical science to determine the formulation of the mind-body problem. Once the body is treated merely as an external object known by physiology, anatomy, and chemistry, and once introspection is thought to reveal mere mind, the original unity of the embodied self is dissolved. Mind and body then appear as two separate existences externally conjoined, and the philosopher asks how they can interact or correlate. Stout traces this mistaken formulation to Descartes and his scientific bias.

Against this, Stout appeals to common sense. In ordinary experience, the psycho-physical problem does not arise as a problem of two separate things. What we are primarily aware of is the individual unity of an embodied self. When I say “I see,” “I hear,” “I handle,” “I walk,” or “I feel a wound,” I cannot replace “I” with either “my body” or “my mind” without changing the meaning.

This is not crude materialism. Stout does not say the body alone thinks, feels, attends, or wills. Nor does he say that a pure ego uses the body as an external instrument. When I turn my eyes, the acting self is not a mere mind acting upon a mere body; it is a more inward and central part of the embodied self initiating bodily action. Even when I imagine, desire, or deliberate about future situations, the self remains essentially embodied and contrasted with a world of external objects and other embodied selves.

Nor are subjective processes spatially located in the crude sense. Stout insists that thinking, willing, perceiving, appetite, desiring, imagining, feeling, attention, and conation are not themselves extended or spatially located. The localization involved is localization of experienced embodiment, not localization of a disembodied mental act in physical space.

The embodied self is therefore a primitive unity, not a compound constructed out of a pure mind and a pure body. Reflective analysis can distinguish bodily and mental aspects, but the living datum is the embodied person.

13. The mind-body problem: Stout’s final treatment

Stout’s mature treatment of the psycho-physical problem is subtle. He rejects materialism, but he does not simply replace it with Cartesian interactionism.

He first formulates the standard problem as the relation between mental and neural series: do they merely vary together, do they mutually determine each other, or is one dependent on the other? But he immediately notes that mental occurrences are fundamentally unlike neural occurrences. His critique of metaphysical materialism is that it makes mind generated by merely bodily factors, thereby excluding genuine mental agency. If bodily processes generate volition and also cause the bodily movement, then the volition itself is not the cause of the movement in the common-sense sense.

He also argues that materialism cannot coherently appeal to emergent evolution if it has already excluded experience from its ultimate materials. Experiencing is essentially disparate from merely material happenings. If purely material conditions are supposed to give rise to mind, the transition is abrupt and unmediated.

Yet Stout’s final solution is not that an immaterial mind interacts with a material body. He says the usual problem admits no satisfactory answer because it rests on a false assumption. Self-consciousness reveals not mere mind but mind and body together in the inseparable unity of the embodied self. The supposed contrast between purely mental and purely bodily process is in part a contrast between the body as external object and the same body as internal object.

Once we begin from embodied self-consciousness, the unity of body and mind does not have to be produced by interaction. The body-mind connection can be accepted as an ultimate datum. Stout says that mental process, as it actually occurs, is also bodily; mental action is also bodily action, and mental passivity is bodily passivity. He even says it is hard to see how mind and body could have the relative independence required for interaction, if by interaction we mean interaction between two already separate things.

This is one of Stout’s most important philosophical moves. He preserves mental irreducibility and agency while rejecting the Cartesian picture of two substances that must somehow communicate. In modern terms, his position is closer to an embodied, non-reductive, anti-Cartesian realism than to either substance dualism or physicalism.

14. Psychophysical correlation and the limits of neuroscience

Stout does not reject psychophysical correlation. He thinks the scientific standpoint has real advantages: physiology gives abstract, schematic, exact, and comprehensive knowledge of the body as an external object. But if science tries to answer the mind-body question wholly from its own external standpoint, it encounters difficulties that can be met only by recognizing the immediate unity of body and mind in self-consciousness.

The proper range of psychophysical correlation, on Stout’s account, is between the body as external object and the bodily aspect of the embodied self. It also includes sensation and active tendency, because these are the aspects of the individual through which he participates in the world process. But thought is different. Knowledge cannot be resolved into mere sensation; active tendency without thought is blind; and the individual would not be a self without thought. Still, Stout says thought does not need a separate psychophysical correlate varying as its object varies. Thought always has a foothold in actual experience, but it is not an independent process requiring its own distinct neural correlate.

This is an important limit on reductionism. Stout does not deny that mental life is correlated with the organism. He denies that every intentional difference in thought must be matched by a special neural item corresponding to that object as such. Thought transcends immediate experience, and its object-reference cannot be treated as a mere neural occurrence.

15. Other minds, intersubjectivity, and social cognition

Stout’s position on other minds is complex. In Mind and Matter, he explicitly says that one mind’s knowledge of another is inferential rather than immediate. But he rejects the standard idea that this inference is merely an argument from analogy based on resemblance between another body and one’s own. The essential factor is responsive behavior. The infant’s experience of something that responds appropriately to hunger, satisfaction, approach, withdrawal, and practical need is more fundamental than a formal analogy between bodies. Intersubjective verification also matters.

At the same time, Stout does not allow the individual mind to begin as an isolated private subject and then somehow infer its way to others. He says that if the individual’s own mind were treated as a unique isolated fact, it would be doubtful whether the individual could even think of a mind other than his own. This suggests a deeper constitutive role for sociality.

The later God and Nature table of contents indicates that Stout develops this in a stronger direction: one chapter is explicitly titled around the claim that knowledge of other minds is not reached by inference but is involved in self-awareness. The best reconciliation is that Stout distinguishes two levels. At the explicit justificatory level, our knowledge of other particular minds is mediated by bodily and responsive behavior. At the deeper constitutive level, self-awareness is already embedded in a field of embodied selves and cannot be understood as a solipsistic starting point.

This is consistent with his broader view. The self is not a pure inner spectator; it is an embodied agent in a world of other embodied agents. The world of mind is from the start social, expressive, and responsive.

16. Souls, spirits, ghosts, and incorporeal minds

Stout’s remarks on the soul are especially important because they prevent a misleading reading of his anti-materialism as simple Cartesian spiritualism.

In The Groundwork of Psychology, Stout says that it may be closer to ultimate truth to speak of psychical dispositions as unconscious states or modifications of a subject, and to call that subject a soul. But he immediately adds that this conception is not helpful to the psychologist, who has no independent means of knowing the soul in a way useful for psychological explanation. For psychology, “soul” is virtually another name for the total system of psychical dispositions and processes.

In Mind and Matter, Stout is even more explicit against the idea of finite pure immaterial minds. He argues that common-sense beliefs in souls, spirits, and ghosts do not support Cartesian pure mind. Traditional ghosts and spirits are not pure res cogitantes; insofar as they are selves at all, they are embodied selves, even if their embodiment is attenuated, rarefied, or inaccessible to ordinary perception. A purely immaterial soul existing apart from any embodiment is, he says, a conception of professional metaphysics, alien to common sense.

This is decisive. Stout’s anti-materialism should not be read as a doctrine of finite disembodied minds. He does not think the normal or primary concept of a self is an incorporeal substance. The finite self is essentially embodied. If survival after death is possible, his own principles would push toward some form of continued embodiment or embodied-like personal existence, not a bare Cartesian mind. The published front matter of God and Nature shows that Stout’s later metaphysical system includes a section on immortality, but the earlier doctrine already makes clear that survival cannot be understood as the persistence of a purely disembodied mental atom.

This also explains the difference between finite souls and the universal Mind in Stout’s system. He rejects pure finite incorporeal minds as alien to common sense; but he later argues that mind as such must be fundamental in the universe, and that Nature requires grounding in a universal eternal Mind. Those are different claims.

17. Mind as fundamental: anti-materialism and universal Mind

Stout’s metaphysics begins from the inadequacy of materialism. Experiencing, thinking, feeling, valuing, willing, and knowing cannot be generated out of what is defined as purely non-experiential. Materialism either leaves mind as an inexplicable eruption or covertly smuggles in something mind-like.

At the end of Mind and Matter, Stout says that embodied selves are continuous with the finite world in one respect and radically discontinuous from insentient nature in another. They share in the world process, but they do so in a distinctive way: they experience, think, know the world, know themselves as members of it, and feel pleasure and pain in ways that make value possible.

He then draws the metaphysical conclusion: mind must be fundamental in the universe of being, not derivative from anything non-mental. He rejects mind-stuff theories because they fail to account for individual selves, and he rejects monadism because it fails to account for anything beyond isolated centers. He therefore says we are bound to posit a universal and eternal Mind developing and expressing itself in the world of finite and changeable beings called Nature.

He immediately distinguishes two options. One might ascribe universal Mind to Nature itself, treating Nature as a self-contained unity; or one might deny that Nature is the whole of Being and posit a Being beyond and distinct from Nature. Stout favors the latter: this Being must be an eternal and universal Mind giving Nature a character otherwise inexplicable.

God and Nature was intended to complete the inquiry begun in Mind and Matter. Its contents show that Stout proceeds through mind-stuff theories, monadism, Fechner, the transition to universal Mind, the conception of a universal Mind, the dependence of finite individuals on a universal Mind, knowledge of ourselves, other minds and God, idealism and universal Mind, unity of interest, good, evil, God, and immortality.

Thus Stout’s final metaphysics is a form of objective or theistic idealism, but not one that denies the reality of the physical world. Nature is real; embodied selves are real; the physical world is known through sense and activity. But Nature is not metaphysically self-sufficient. Mind is ontologically fundamental.

18. What Stout is not

It is useful to clarify Stout’s position by exclusion.

He is not a reductive materialist. He denies that mind can be generated by merely material conditions and argues that experiencing is fundamentally discontinuous with the purely material as such.

He is not a behaviorist. Behavior, especially responsive behavior, is crucial for knowledge of other selves, but psychical process, experience, attention, conation, feeling, and thought remain indispensable psychological realities.

He is not a Cartesian substance dualist. The primary datum is not a pure mind confronting a separate body; it is the embodied self. The mind-body problem arises from abstraction when the body is treated merely as an external object and self-consciousness is misdescribed as awareness of mere mind.

He is not a pure spiritualist. Stout says that common experience gives no support to the view that attending, feeling, and willing are functions of a pure or transcendental ego.

He is not a simple panpsychist. He rejects mind-stuff theories and monadism as inadequate, though he does hold that mind is fundamental in the universe. His metaphysical endpoint is universal Mind, not a mere distribution of tiny minds throughout matter.

He is not a psychologistic logician. Thought is a psychological act, but the object of thought is not a private mental content. This is why his work becomes historically important for analytic realism and anti-psychologism.

19. The comprehensive philosophy of mind extractable from Stout

The following is the most systematic reconstruction of Stout’s philosophy of mind.

First, mental life is processual. The mind is not a substance first and a process second; psychology studies the flow of psychical process, its forms, stages, conditions, and development. The self is known through the organization and continuity of lived process.

Second, mental life is dispositional. Conscious episodes depend on enduring psychical dispositions: memories, habits, capacities, acquired tendencies, emotional orientations, and latent systems of response. These dispositions are not always conscious, but they structure consciousness.

Third, consciousness is intentional. Mental acts are modes of reference to objects. The object of thought is not identical with the content of consciousness. This is the basis of Stout’s realism about knowledge, judgment, meaning, and propositions.

Fourth, mental unity is noetic rather than merely associative. Percepts, ideas, and concepts are unified by objective reference. Trains of thought are often governed by a central topic or whole, not merely by the last idea that happened to occur.

Fifth, attention is active. It is both object-reference and mental activity: the mind maintains, develops, and privileges one object over others.

Sixth, conation is fundamental. Mind is not primarily a spectator. It strives, tends, seeks, desires, wills, and organizes cognition around ends. Cognition is not external to conation; it articulates conation from within.

Seventh, belief is action-oriented. Belief connects means and ends, supports conduct, and is hindered by doubt. It is not merely inert representation.

Eighth, affect is conative. Pleasure and pain express the success or obstruction of active tendency. They ground the possibility of value, good, and evil.

Ninth, perception is embodied realism. Physical existence is known through sense and activity together. The self knows itself as embodied in the same field in which it knows external resistance, bodily effort, and other embodied selves.

Tenth, the self is essentially embodied. The primary “I” is neither body alone nor mind alone. It is the embodied person who sees, walks, handles, suffers, imagines, desires, and thinks.

Eleventh, psychophysical correlation is real but limited. There are correlations between bodily process and aspects of experience, but thought as object-reference cannot be reduced to a special neural correlate for each object thought about.

Twelfth, finite disembodied minds are not the primary or intelligible model of mind. The soul, for psychology, is only a shorthand for the system of psychical processes and dispositions; pure incorporeal finite souls are metaphysical constructions, not common-sense data.

Thirteenth, mind is metaphysically fundamental. Materialism cannot explain the emergence of experience from what is wholly non-experiential. Stout’s final answer is that Nature is grounded in universal Mind.

This reconstruction shows Stout’s system as a coherent philosophy: analytic psychology leads to intentionality; intentionality leads to realism about objects of thought; conation and attention lead to an active conception of mind; perception and action lead to embodiment; embodiment dissolves the Cartesian formulation of the mind-body problem; anti-materialism leads to the metaphysical priority of mind; and the priority of mind culminates in universal Mind.

20. Stout’s place in the larger development of philosophy of mind

Stout’s historical importance is larger than his present reputation suggests.

In relation to nineteenth-century associationism, he is a critic and reformer. He preserves the empirical spirit of British psychology but rejects the reduction of mental life to associative sequences. His noetic synthesis, attention, and conation introduce organization, purpose, and object-reference into the heart of psychology.

In relation to Brentano and early phenomenology, he is a mediator. He brings the analysis of intentionality, object-reference, and descriptive psychology into British philosophy, but modifies Brentano by sharpening the distinction between content and object.

In relation to British idealism, he is both heir and critic. He shares the idealist suspicion of materialism and eventually develops a metaphysics of universal Mind. But he is also a common-sense realist about the objectivity of thought, the external world, embodied selves, and ordinary experience. The review of van der Schaar’s work aptly characterizes him as an “uncompromisingly common-sense psychologist” whose later metaphysics complements rather than simply replaces his earlier descriptive psychology.

In relation to early analytic philosophy, he is a neglected source. Moore and Russell’s movement toward analytic realism did not emerge only from logic or from Frege. Stout’s act/content/object distinction and his insistence on the objectivity of thought helped create the background for their anti-psychologistic theories of judgment and proposition.

In relation to the twentieth-century mind-body debate, Stout anticipates several later concerns without fitting neatly into later categories. His embodied self is anti-Cartesian; his critique of reductive materialism anticipates later worries about emergence and the explanatory gap; his dispositional psychology anticipates non-occurrent mental states; his attention to action and conation anticipates action-oriented and enactive themes; and his noetic synthesis anticipates holistic accounts of cognition. But he is not simply a precursor of contemporary physicalism, functionalism, phenomenology, or embodied cognition. His final metaphysics is theistic and idealist in a way most contemporary philosophy of mind is not.

21. Final assessment

Stout’s philosophy of mind is a remarkably integrated system. It begins with the analysis of ordinary mental life and ends with a metaphysics of universal Mind. Its central insight is that mind must be understood as an embodied, object-directed, active, dispositional, developing process. The self is not a ghost in a machine, not a machine producing a ghost, and not a bundle of sensations. It is an embodied agent whose consciousness is organized by objective reference, sustained by dispositions, driven by conation, guided by attention, articulated through thought and language, affectively attuned to success and failure, and situated among other embodied selves in a world of physical resistance and shared activity.

His comments on the soul are therefore not peripheral. They clarify the whole architecture. At the level of psychology, “soul” is not an explanatory substance but the system of psychical processes and dispositions. At the level of common-sense anthropology, finite selves are embodied; even traditional spirits and ghosts are imagined as embodied in some attenuated way. At the metaphysical level, however, Stout does affirm that mind is fundamental in being and that Nature requires grounding in a universal eternal Mind. The finite mind is embodied; the universe is ultimately mind-grounded. That is the distinctive tension and ambition of Stout’s philosophy of mind.

PreviousCarl Stumpf NextKazimierz Twardowski

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
